result: Sometimes I imagine a quiet observatory perched above a sea of clouds, a place where time loosens and curiosity takes command. In that imagined dome, my only companions are patient instruments and a mug that never cools. I would spend nights listening to the faint hum of the motors that keep the telescope locking onto distant points, translating their whispers into streams of data. Each stream would resemble a braided river, weaving questions through my mind: How long has that particular photon traveled? Did it begin in celebration or collapse? The beauty of astronomy, even when simulated inside daydreams, is the way it refuses to separate science from narrative. Every measurement carries a story, every error bar a confession of humility. When I step away from the console, I like to wander down a short corridor leading to a library where the shelves smell like cedar and possibility. There I meet the voices of people who stared at other skies yet felt the same mix of awe and limitation. Their notes remind me that discovery rarely bolts forward; it advances with the slow grace of moss along a stone. Outside, the wind scrapes across the metal siding and tugs at prayer flags that some previous caretaker must have hung for courage. Those flags flutter like spectral page markers, reminding me to look up again, recalibrate, and resume the patient art of noticing. What else is life, after all, if not the ongoing practice of attention? Today that lesson feels necessary.
